definition: Layer of skin beneath epidermis containing blood vessels and nerves
rationale: The dermis is the second layer of skin containing blood vessels nerve endings hair follicles and sweat glands. It provides structural support and sensation. Damage to dermis causes bleeding and pain.

definition: Fat layer beneath the dermis
rationale: Subcutaneous tissue or hypodermis is the fatty layer beneath the dermis. It provides insulation energy storage and cushioning. Deep wounds or burns extending to this layer are more serious.

definition: Voluntary muscle attached to bones for movement
rationale: Skeletal muscle is under voluntary control and attaches to bones via tendons. Contraction produces body movement. It appears striated under microscope and fatigues with prolonged use.

definition: Specialized heart muscle that contracts automatically
rationale: Cardiac muscle is found only in the heart. It contracts automatically and rhythmically without conscious control. It is striated like skeletal muscle but has unique properties allowing coordinated contraction.

definition: Involuntary muscle in organs and blood vessels
rationale: Smooth muscle is involuntary muscle found in blood vessel walls airways digestive tract and other organs. It contracts slowly and can maintain tone. It is not striated under microscope.

definition: Connective tissue attaching muscle to bone
rationale: Tendons are tough fibrous connective tissue that attach muscles to bones. When muscle contracts it pulls on the tendon which moves the bone. Tendon injuries affect movement.

definition: Connective tissue connecting bone to bone
rationale: Ligaments connect bones to other bones at joints providing stability. Sprains are ligament injuries. Ligaments limit joint motion and prevent abnormal movement.

definition: Flexible connective tissue in joints and other structures
rationale: Cartilage is smooth flexible connective tissue covering bone ends in joints cushioning the ear and nose and forming airway structures. It reduces friction and absorbs shock.

definition: Upper portion of the sternum
rationale: The manubrium is the upper portion of the sternum articulating with the clavicles and first ribs. The suprasternal notch is the depression at its top between the clavicles.

definition: Small cartilaginous extension at bottom of sternum
rationale: The xiphoid process is the small cartilaginous projection at the inferior end of the sternum. It is a landmark for hand placement during CPR and can be damaged by improper compressions.

definition: Lower edge of the rib cage
rationale: The costal margin is the lower border of the thoracic cage formed by the cartilages of ribs 7-10. It is a landmark for abdominal examination dividing upper from lower abdomen.

definition: Upper border of the hip bone
rationale: The iliac crest is the curved upper border of the ilium the largest pelvic bone. It is palpable at the waist and serves as a landmark for positioning and splinting.

definition: Bony prominence on lateral upper femur
rationale: The greater trochanter is the bony prominence palpable on the lateral hip. It is a site for femur fracture assessment and serves as a landmark for hip positioning.

definition: Bony prominences on each side of ankle
rationale: The malleoli are the bony prominences on each side of the ankle. The medial malleolus is part of the tibia and the lateral malleolus is part of the fibula. Both are commonly injured.

definition: Point of the elbow formed by the ulna
rationale: The olecranon is the bony point of the elbow formed by the proximal ulna. It is commonly injured in falls and is the site where the triceps tendon inserts.

definition: Artery carrying deoxygenated blood from heart to lungs
rationale: The pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood from the right ventricle to the lungs for oxygenation. It is the only artery carrying deoxygenated blood. It divides into right and left branches.

definition: Vein carrying oxygenated blood from lungs to heart
rationale: Pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood from the lungs to the left atrium. They are the only veins carrying oxygenated blood. Four pulmonary veins enter the left atrium.

definition: Wall separating right and left sides of heart
rationale: The septum is the muscular wall dividing the heart into right and left sides. The interatrial septum separates the atria and the interventricular septum separates the ventricles preventing blood mixing.

definition: Valve between right atrium and right ventricle
rationale: The tricuspid valve has three leaflets and controls blood flow from the right atrium to the right ventricle. It prevents backflow during ventricular contraction.

definition: Valve between left atrium and left ventricle
rationale: The mitral or bicuspid valve has two leaflets and controls blood flow from the left atrium to the left ventricle. It prevents backflow during ventricular contraction.

definition: Valve between left ventricle and aorta
rationale: The aortic valve controls blood flow from the left ventricle into the aorta. It opens during ventricular contraction and closes during relaxation to prevent backflow.

definition: Valve between right ventricle and pulmonary artery
rationale: The pulmonic valve controls blood flow from the right ventricle into the pulmonary artery. It opens during ventricular contraction and closes to prevent backflow.

definition: Heart's primary pacemaker in right atrium
rationale: The sinoatrial SA node is the heart's natural pacemaker located in the right atrium. It generates electrical impulses that spread through the heart causing coordinated contraction at 60-100 bpm.

definition: Electrical relay point between atria and ventricles
rationale: The atrioventricular AV node receives impulses from the SA node and delays them briefly before passing to the ventricles. This delay allows atrial contraction to complete before ventricular contraction.

definition: Twelve pairs of nerves arising directly from the brain
rationale: The 12 pairs of cranial nerves originate from the brain and brainstem controlling functions including vision eye movement facial sensation and movement hearing and swallowing.

definition: Nerves arising from the spinal cord
rationale: Thirty-one pairs of spinal nerves exit the spinal cord carrying sensory information in and motor commands out. They are named for the vertebral level where they exit.

definition: Nerves outside the brain and spinal cord
rationale: The PNS includes all nerves outside the CNS including cranial nerves spinal nerves and their branches. It connects the CNS to the rest of the body.

definition: Brain and spinal cord
rationale: The CNS consists of the brain and spinal cord. It processes information and coordinates responses. It is protected by bone meninges and cerebrospinal fluid.

definition: Nerves carrying signals from CNS to muscles
rationale: Motor or efferent neurons carry signals from the CNS to muscles and glands causing movement and other responses. Damage causes weakness or paralysis.

definition: Nerves carrying signals from body to CNS
rationale: Sensory or afferent neurons carry information from sensory receptors to the CNS. They transmit touch pain temperature and position sense. Damage causes numbness.

definition: Tube connecting throat to stomach
rationale: The esophagus is a muscular tube approximately 10 inches long connecting the pharynx to the stomach. It passes behind the trachea and heart through the diaphragm to the stomach.

definition: Organ that stores and begins digestion of food
rationale: The stomach is a J-shaped organ in the left upper quadrant that stores food secretes acid and enzymes and mechanically breaks down food. It can hold approximately 1 liter.

definition: Long tube where most nutrient absorption occurs
rationale: The small intestine is approximately 20 feet long and is where most digestion and nutrient absorption occurs. It has three sections: duodenum jejunum and ileum.

definition: Colon where water absorption and waste storage occurs
rationale: The large intestine or colon is approximately 5 feet long and absorbs water and electrolytes from digestive waste. It stores feces until elimination. It includes the cecum colon and rectum.

definition: Organ storing bile produced by the liver
rationale: The gallbladder is a small sac beneath the liver that stores and concentrates bile. Bile is released into the duodenum to help digest fats. Gallstones can cause pain and obstruction.

definition: Tubes carrying urine from kidneys to bladder
rationale: The ureters are muscular tubes approximately 10 inches long that transport urine from each kidney to the bladder by peristalsis. Kidney stones can obstruct them causing severe pain.

definition: Organ storing urine until elimination
rationale: The urinary bladder is a muscular sac that stores urine. It can hold 300-500 mL comfortably. Bladder trauma can occur with pelvic fractures especially when full.

definition: Tube carrying urine from bladder to outside
rationale: The urethra is the tube that carries urine from the bladder to the outside of the body. It is longer in males than females. Urethral injury can occur with pelvic trauma.

definition: Female organ where fetus develops during pregnancy
rationale: The uterus or womb is a pear-shaped muscular organ in the female pelvis where a fertilized egg implants and the fetus develops. It expands greatly during pregnancy.

definition: Organ connecting fetus to uterine wall providing nutrients
rationale: The placenta develops during pregnancy and attaches to the uterine wall. It allows exchange of oxygen nutrients and waste between mother and fetus via the umbilical cord.

definition: Connection between fetus and placenta
rationale: The umbilical cord connects the fetus to the placenta containing two arteries and one vein. It transports oxygenated blood and nutrients to the fetus and waste away.

definition: Small organs filtering lymph fluid
rationale: Lymph nodes are small bean-shaped organs that filter lymph fluid removing pathogens and abnormal cells. They are located throughout the body and swell during infection.

definition: Vessels carrying lymph fluid throughout the body
rationale: Lymphatic vessels transport lymph fluid from tissues back to the bloodstream. They parallel blood vessels and pass through lymph nodes. The lymphatic system aids immunity.

definition: Gland in neck controlling metabolism
rationale: The thyroid gland is located in the anterior neck and produces hormones regulating metabolism. Thyroid emergencies include thyroid storm hyperthyroidism and myxedema coma hypothyroidism.

definition: Glands on kidneys producing stress hormones
rationale: The adrenal glands sit atop each kidney and produce hormones including cortisol and epinephrine. They are essential for stress response. Adrenal crisis is a life-threatening emergency.

definition: Master gland in brain controlling other glands
rationale: The pituitary gland at the base of the brain produces hormones that control other endocrine glands. It regulates growth metabolism and reproduction among other functions.

definition: Eardrum separating outer and middle ear
rationale: The tympanic membrane or eardrum is a thin membrane that vibrates in response to sound waves. It separates the external ear canal from the middle ear. Rupture causes hearing loss.

definition: Clear front surface of the eye
rationale: The cornea is the transparent front portion of the eye that covers the iris and pupil. It refracts light and protects the eye. Corneal abrasions are painful common injuries.

definition: Light-sensitive layer at back of eye
rationale: The retina lines the back of the eye and contains photoreceptors that convert light to nerve signals. These signals travel via the optic nerve to the brain for vision processing.

definition: Opening in center of iris controlling light entry
rationale: The pupil is the circular opening in the center of the iris. It constricts in bright light and dilates in darkness. Pupil size and reaction are important neurological assessment findings.
